attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department after vomiting approximately two cupfuls of fresh blood this morning. He also reports abdominal pain that has been worsening over the past two weeks. His medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes. His surgical history includes an abdominal aortic aneurysm repair seven years previously and coronary artery bypass surgery two years ago. His medications include perindopril, metformin and aspirin. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and drinks three glasses of wine a day.

On examination his blood pressure is 100/60mmHg and pulse 100/min. Focused abdominal examination reveals some generalised tenderness in his abdomen. There are no other abnormal findings.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following conditions must be excluded first?
attributes.explanation:
When a patient presents with an acute upper gastrointestinal bleed, the common and most likely causes would be uppermost in the clinician's mind. In cases such as described here, peptic ulcer disease and oesophageal varices would be very likely causes. However, what the clinician should also be considering is any other cause that could have rapid and life-threatening deterioration in the patient's condition. This patient has had previous abdominal aortic aneurysm surgery and could have a complication related to this. Until an aortoenteric fistula is excluded, this must be the working diagnosis.

Aortoenteric fistula is a rare clinical entity but one that carries an exceptionally high mortality rate. This patient's presentation could represent the so called 'herald bleed' where initial haemorrhage occurs and spontaneously stops. A period of relative stability follows lasting a variable length of time, likely due to the formation of a temporising clot. When this clot fails, massive exsanguination follows.

A high index of clinical suspicion and low threshold for performing a CT aortogram in upper gastrointestinal bleeding is required in patients with a history of aortic surgery/prosthetic aortic graft. This ensures prompt diagnosis of an aortoenteric fistula, which is paramount to reducing mortality through timely intervention.

A gastric, duodenal ulcer, Dieulafoy's lesion, and oesophageal varices would all be more common causes of this patient's presentation. They would require resuscitation and possibly an endoscopy to control bleeding.
learningPoints: A high index of suspicion of aortoenteric fistula is required for patients who present with gastrointestinal bleeding on a background of abdominal aortic aneurysm repair.
references: Busuttil SJ, Goldstone J. Diagnosis and management of aortoenteric fistulas. Semin Vasc Surg. 2001 Dec;14(4):302-11. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11740838/#:~:text=Symptomatology%20can%20be%20varied%20but,the%20infected%20graft%20and%20revascularization.

Vu QD, Menias CO, Bhalla S, Peterson C, Wang LL, et al. Aortoenteric fistulas: CT features and potential mimics. Radiographics. 2009;29(1):197-209. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19168845/#:~:text=To%20use%20this%20modality%20effectively,and%20perigraft%20infection%20without%20fistulization.
Baxter M, Aly EH. Dieulafoy's lesion: Current trends in diagnosis and management. Ann R Coll Surg Engl. 2010;92(7):548-54. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3229341/
